questions.question: When did the French Revolution begin with the storming of the Bastille?
questions.explanation: The Bastille, a fortress-prison, was hated by all because it stood for the despotic power of the king[cite: 201].

questions.question: Who was the King of France during the outbreak of the Revolution?
questions.correct_answer: Louis XVI of the Bourbon family[cite: 203].
questions.explanation: He ascended the throne in 1774 and found an empty treasury due to long years of war and the cost of maintaining an extravagant court at Versailles[cite: 203].

questions.question: Describe the 'Old Regime' social structure in France.
questions.correct_answer: Society was divided into three estates: the Clergy, the Nobility, and the Third Estate[cite: 203, 204].
questions.explanation: Only members of the third estate, who made up about 90% of the population, paid taxes[cite: 203, 204].

questions.question: What were the 'tithes' and 'taille'?
questions.correct_answer: Tithes were taxes levied by the Church; Taille was a tax paid directly to the state[cite: 204].
questions.explanation: These financial burdens fell entirely on the Third Estate[cite: 204].

questions.question: What was the Tennis Court Oath?
questions.correct_answer: A pledge by the Third Estate to not disperse until they had drafted a constitution for France[cite: 207].
questions.explanation: They declared themselves a National Assembly on 20 June 1789 in the indoor tennis court at Versailles[cite: 207].

questions.question: What document did the National Assembly adopt in 1789 to outline citizen rights?
questions.correct_answer: The Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen[cite: 209].
questions.explanation: It established rights such as liberty, property, security, and resistance to oppression as 'natural and inalienable'[cite: 211].

questions.question: Who were the Jacobins, and who was their leader?
questions.correct_answer: A radical political club led by Maximilian Robespierre[cite: 213, 214].
questions.explanation: They represented the less prosperous sections of society and were instrumental in the fall of the monarchy[cite: 213, 214].

questions.question: What was the 'Reign of Terror'?
questions.correct_answer: A period from 1793 to 1794 characterized by Robespierre's policy of severe control and punishment[cite: 215].
questions.explanation: Anyone seen as an 'enemy' of the republic was arrested, tried, and if found guilty, guillotined[cite: 215].

questions.question: When was slavery finally abolished in all French colonies?
questions.explanation: Though the Convention legislated to free slaves in 1794, Napoleon reintroduced it ten years later[cite: 220].

questions.question: Who rose to power following the fall of the Directory?
questions.explanation: He crowned himself Emperor of France in 1804 and saw himself as a moderniser of Europe[cite: 216, 222].

notes.title: The Three Estates
notes.content: France's feudal society was split: 1st Estate (Clergy), 2nd Estate (Nobility), and 3rd Estate (Big businessmen, merchants, peasants, artisans)[cite: 204].

notes.title: The Subsistence Crisis
notes.content: An extreme situation where basic means of livelihood are endangered, caused in France by rapid population growth, poor harvests, and rising bread prices[cite: 204, 205].

notes.title: Women in the Revolution
notes.content: Women were active participants, forming their own political clubs (e.g., The Society of Revolutionary and Republican Women) and demanding equal political rights[cite: 217, 218].

facts.fact: On 21 September 1792, the newly elected assembly, called the Convention, abolished the monarchy and declared France a Republic[cite: 214].
Louis XVI was executed publicly at the Place de la Concorde on 21 January 1793[cite: 214].
The guillotine was a device consisting of two poles and a blade with which a person is beheaded, named after Dr. Guillotin[cite: 215].
Napoleon Bonaparte was finally defeated at the Battle of Waterloo in 1815[cite: 222].
